slides.title: Measuring Deployment Characteristics of PQ TLS Authentication Mechanisms
Akamu ACME server Hackathon interop results report

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: batteries not included

chat.author: Rich Salz
chat.text: The "extra" chair on the stage is leftover from the previous session to accomodate a presenter who had physical issues. It could be removed and the mic stand put back into the common position.

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: ACME today? Isn't that Thursday?

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: yes, it's thursday.

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
"Log number encoded in serial
● 16-bit log number, 48-bit entry index"
Didn't PKI move to randomized serial numbers as the obvious point to inject entropy into the TBSCertificate?
Is this design point addressed in the draft?

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: That's the randomizer.

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: Only relevant if the hash is weak (which it isn't), or if you want to eek out more than 128-bit security from a 256-bit hash. https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/plants/FN8L2-C2AjN7QxpX6RC7b3tnxi8/

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
draft-ietf-plants-merkle-tree-certs-05 has 0 CTRL+F hits for "randomizer". Is that an addition that's not in the draft yet?
What I'm looking for is the details: is randomizer an X.509v3 extn in the TBSCert of the leaf entry?

chat.author: Brendan McMillion
chat.text: Randomizer isn't built-in. It's a to-be-written extension

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: relative OID would be my preference as well, fwiw.

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: The bikeshed question is not about the color. It's about whether we make the bike shed of galvanized iron or aluminum.  (actually aluminium because the book is British)

chat.author: Aaron Gable
chat.text: Do we have any upki representatives to speak to their implementation plan / timeline?

chat.author: Viktor Dukhovni
chat.text: @bob-beck signal chat me...

chat.author: Deb Cooley
chat.text: @aarongable - mic?

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: Re randomizer, yeah, the mailing list discussion was inconclusive, so extensions are a way to strategically kick the can down the road. :-)

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
David Benjamin said:

Re randomizer, yeah, the mailing list discussion was inconclusive, so extensions are a way to strategically kick the can down the road. :-)

I'm asking to see the details so I can noodle on whether that's security-equivalent to randomized Serial.

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: Did you read the thread, Mike? :)

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: Details would just be to allocate extension zero to have contents "16 random bytes" and you put that in every entry.

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
Bas Westerbaan said:

Did you read the thread, Mike? :)

Yes, I see that it has more replies than I can read while I'm setting here.
Am asking whether there is a section in the draft with the current proposal. Yes, or no?

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: instructions unclear, put "16 random bytes" into the extension

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: No, not in the current proposal.

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: Happy to walk you through the details later.

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: And of course we can add text to the security considerations if you feel it's not obvious

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text:
Re putting it in the serial, previously, we put randomizers in the serial number just because:

It's a field that already exists
Unlike extensions, it's in front, and it's kinda nice to put it in the front

But for MTC, we're defining a new structure that is being hashed so we can just define it to do whatever we want. So we're not forced to jam it into the serial.

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text: (I have now skimmed the email thread. I didn't see an obvious block of ASN.1 defn's + MUST's / SHOULD's. Is that in the email thread and I just missed it?)

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: Appeal to git is a novel strategy for pitching simplicity

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: plus, git rebase is an incredible opportunity for concealing shennanigans in dev history

chat.author: Stefan Santesson
chat.text: What prevents the act of reconciling a log from creating new forks that needs to be reconciled?

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: If mirrors get stuck, it may become an emergency due to paused issuance. If monitors get stuck, its a temporary loss of observability which is resolved when the monitor is fixed.

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: I've spent way too long resolving conflicts from git rebase.

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: That suggests to me that keeping the mirrors simpler might be the way to go.

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: anyone planning any formal methods work on plants related things? eg. this stuff

chat.author: Devon O'Brien
chat.text: I'm honestly a lot more interested in building ecosystem capability and agility to respond to a hosed log instead of introducing a complex graph-theoretic class of failure modes to an already error-prone ecosystem.

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: Fortunately, if we're wrong and this does turn out to be super easy (I'm dubious), we can set maxSerial to only allow one log and now log generations are a no-op.

chat.author: Bob Beck
chat.text: presumably of course the CA won't be a jerk.

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: plus, there are (at least) two different ways to do any reconciliation.  are we going to fork based on which reconciliation choice was made?

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
Stephen Farrell said:

anyone planning any formal methods work on plants related things? eg. this stuff

Yeah. How do we convince ourselves that we've sufficiently considered all the edge-cases that need to be considered? Are we there already? How do we know?

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: @MikeO:  Is that by its nature, open ended?

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: Is it really deterministic?  Or non-deterministic.

chat.author: Bob Beck
chat.text:
Ooops we had a bug in our CA was auto forking the tree, but reconcillation made it invisible.. 
until the log fell over.

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text: @BobMoskowitz -- Yes, but for example TLS 1.3 design + formal methods goes a long way to at least attempting to reach "completeness". I'm just musing out loud about what the equivalent bar is for the MTC design.

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: I do think we need the arch draft pronto. If only to concisely set out what the system is intended to achieve (when deployed 'correctly'). That will give the formal methods folks something to shoot at.

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: Yeah, some manual friction in generations is actually a feature not a bug.

chat.author: Russ Housley
chat.text: With major decisions behind us, an arch doc now seems do-able

chat.author: Stefan Santesson
chat.text: What happens if a certificate gets stuck in a fork that eventually is abandoned, does that not just cause the certificate to fail validation. Does this needs to be fixed?

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: it should still be auditable that this stuck certificate ever existed

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: The timelines I recall for the TLSv1.3 analyses might impact on plants milestones. More importantly might conflict with announcements related to plants and timelines.

chat.author: Peter van Dijk
chat.text: Total backseat driver here. How about something closer to git merge than git rebase? That gives us something visible

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: i like Aaron's "merge log entry" more than the rebase.

chat.author: Stefan Santesson
chat.text: And why ia that a problem. Not saying that it isn't

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: @Stefan: the first goal here transparency, to ensure CAs behave and don't create certificates for people they shouldn't be signing for

chat.author: Stefan Santesson
chat.text: OK. Thanks.

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: ISTM that pairwise could still flipflop because rebase(A, B) != rebase(B, A).

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text:
image.png
Shameless self citation for what transparency means

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: davidben realising some issue at the mic => maybe that formal analysis stuff's needed before deployment?

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
"What if the same serial number exists on two forks?"
--> This is a problem introduced by removing strong random serial numbers, right? :thinking: 
I would very much like to see the full proposal for non-random serials written out because I think the details matter!

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: So the "invisible same serial number" problem doesn't exist right now. The log generations strategy (intentionally!) makes it very visible.

chat.author: Felix Linker
chat.text:
Stephen Farrell said:

davidben realising some issue at the mic => maybe that formal analysis stuff's needed before deployment?

I would not advise to add a dependency on formal analysis :innocent: Though, formal analysis is definitely desirable

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: does a new generation point to "this old generation was retired at point X"?

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text:
I would very much like to see the full proposal for non-random serials written out because I think the details matter!

I haven't heard anyone propose before to reintroduce random serial numbers. Random serial numbers were a hack. We shouldn't bring them back as-is.

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: @felix: yeah it's tricky given the announced timelines. OTOH, discovering badness later could make us quite sad

chat.author: Aaron Gable
chat.text: @Thom Wiggers No, but it could with a new log entry type. And that might help resolve the "what if a CA tries to continue appending to a previous log generation" concern.

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
Bas Westerbaan said:

I haven't heard anyone propose before to reintroduce random serial numbers. Random serial numbers were a hack. We shouldn't bring them back as-is.

Sure, and I'm not proposing to bring them back. I'm proposing that doing a differential security analysis relative to the properties of the X.509 that we've studied to death requires having the precise details of the new thing written down.

chat.author: Rich Salz
chat.text: I'd be interested in (helping) with the arch document if everyone else is busy

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: Perhaps a different proposal to consider: have an optional entry type to be added on a new log generation that contains the hash (or hashes) of the previous generation (forks.)

chat.author: Rich Salz
chat.text: I do kinda like the reconciliation thing if it means we get log entry types '<<<<<<' etc

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: Rich :joy:

chat.author: Bob Beck
chat.text: pki rebase --hard

chat.text:
I was worried we would not get any bad plant puns
thank god someone remembered to keep their presentation up to standards.

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: I feel very silly now for forgetting haha.

chat.author: Aaron Gable
chat.text: Is the idea here that the LDP is baked into the cert via extension, like the CRLDP today?

chat.text: puns:PLANTS :: dadjokes:dads ?

chat.text: is plants overgrown into saag?

chat.author: Bob Beck
chat.text: and some people were annoyed at me for naming a draft leafy greens....

chat.text: certificate hierarchies and network availability: CHANA SAAG

chat.author: Aritra Banerjee
chat.text: Now that's creative!

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: John might kill Russ with those puns

chat.author: Bob Beck
chat.text: +11 for puns.

chat.author: Jonathan Hammell
chat.text: Will those draft branches be rebased in the future?

chat.author: Bob Beck
chat.text: you mean pruned and grafted...

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: I can answer how it's useful for downgrade detection. It's important to distinguish two kind of classical certs: the one to show a legacy client and the one to provision at a legacy server (that can't sign with PQ). It's only the latter case that causes a downgrade, but also you only need to issue that one if you have a legacy server. That does require a misissuance, but that can be detected with transparency.

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: @Aaron Gable You can do even better compression with non-random serial numbers in MTC: https://github.com/bwesterb/go-ncrlite

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: saag will also talk a bit about this. https://www.chromium.org/Home/chromium-security/post-quantum-auth-roadmap/ has a write-up, but saag will have even more fun pictures. :-)

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text:
Bas Westerbaan said:

Aaron Gable You can do even better compression with non-random serial numbers in MTC: https://github.com/bwesterb/go-ncrlite

Yeah, it really feels like we could do nicer things with revocation now that our serials dense and ordered. Maybe size savings, maybe more natural sharding for CAs that want to keep their CDN pushes small, maybe something else.

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: (Isn't a plain counter how serials originally worked? I thought making them random was a later development.)

chat.author: Deb Cooley
chat.text: This is true.

chat.author: Deb Cooley
chat.text: It was added as a result of a hash that was less secure than we liked.

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: That was the RapidSSL MD5 thing, right?

chat.author: Russ Housley
chat.text: The random was added to the serial because of the Bleichenbacher Attack

chat.author: Deb Cooley
chat.text: To make the 'next certificate' issued be less predictable.

chat.author: Russ Housley
chat.text: Yep

chat.author: Aaron Gable
chat.text:
In summary: x509v3 still doesn't require serial randomness; the CA/BF Baseline Requirements require it. It was introduced in 2016 (Ballot 164) as a result of chosen-prefix attacks against MD5.
https://cabforum.org/2016/03/31/ballot-164-certificate-serial-number-entropy/

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: Yeah, because RapidSSL used a serial serial number, so the attackers could predict (and manipulate by requesting certificates) the serial number of the rogue certificate.

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: Those numbers are actually quite a bit better than I expected.

chat.author: Devon O'Brien
chat.text: It was also sheer luck that serial number was in the right place in the certificate (i.e. before the key) and had the right format/shape/purpose to store some entropy

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text:
Bas Westerbaan said:

Those numbers are actually quite a bit better than I expected.

Still a terrifying long way to go.

chat.author: Devon O'Brien
chat.text: This was not a principled decision by any means

chat.author: Deb Cooley
chat.text: right.  The other option was to add random to the fast moving parts of the 'not before' and 'not after'.

chat.author: Deb Cooley
chat.text: (was my pref)

chat.author: Josh G
chat.text: hard to know if what's been migrated is what's easiest to migrate, or the most important

chat.author: Aaron Gable
chat.text: Heh, sorry, Let's Encrypt is "doing this four certificate business" right now. It's necessary for any CA which is trying to support both old (trusted by out-of-date clients only) and new (trusted by up-to-date clients only) roots simultaneously.

chat.author: Bob Beck
chat.text: in private pki (speaking from a consultant role in past lives) four and more is usually some form of encoding your org chart into your certificate chain.

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: How to deal with old vs new clients, the perennial PKI problem. :-)

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: perennial IETF problem

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: "the curse of the deployed base"

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: "What are these people doing in my tent? How do I get them to leave? What will happen to them if they are chucked out?"

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: (overheard at the IETF)

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: it's usually more like "what will happen to my tent if they are chucked out?"

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
Deb Cooley said:

To make the 'next certificate' issued be less predictable.

I'm curious to think through the implications of being able to predict a future not-yet-issued leaf of the Merkle tree -- ie what is the bad thing that happens if you don't have randomness in the MTC leaf? My intuition is that the bad thing here is quite a bit different from the bad thing in traditional X.509 (which largely boils down to RSA / ECDSA hash collisions).

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: It's ultimately the same thing: how strong do you believe your hash is. If your hash is collision-resistant, it's fine.

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: One hopes we're no longer playing with MD5 anymore. For SHA-256... if a 256-bit hash does not give you enough bits of collision resistance for you, then maybe you want the randomizer.

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: agreed, the only problem motivating randomized IDs was MD5's collision-resistance failure

chat.author: Aaron Gable
chat.text: Also, you'd have to predict the entire checkpoint subtree, not just a single entry.

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
David Benjamin said:

It's ultimately the same thing: how strong do you believe your hash is. If your hash is collision-resistant, it's fine.

I don't follow this. If I know the exact value of a future MTC leaf, then I run the hash _forward_ to build an inclusion proof for this not-yet-issued leaf, which is a thing that my intuition tells me shouldn't be good. Since this is not a collision attack, I'm not following how using a collision-resistant hash protects you?

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: (Re number of bits, personally, I don't really believe in counting to 2^128. I have a low attention span and will get bored before then. But opinions vary.)

chat.author: Andrew Dolan
chat.text: Along with the idea of a deployment guidance, and perhaps to be paired with the use cases draft, I'd like to see a good side by side breakdown of MTC sizes, particularly between where you do and don't have landmark relative certs. IIRC the MTC draft has some of this started already (estimates)

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text:
Mike Ounsworth said:

David Benjamin said:

It's ultimately the same thing: how strong do you believe your hash is. If your hash is collision-resistant, it's fine.

I don't follow this. If I know the exact value of a future MTC leaf, then I run the hash _forward_ to build an inclusion proof for this not-yet-issued leaf, which is a thing that my intuition tells me shouldn't be good. And I don't think that requires collision-resistance of the hash?

If you could collide the hash and control attacker.example, you can find a colliding pair A and B, where A has SANs attacker.example and B has SANs target.example. You present the first one to the CA, get them to sign it, but actually that certificate also certifies B. And now you've caused the CA to certify an unauthorized cert for target.example.

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
Sure. Collision attacks against MTC are clearly bad in exactly the same way as for traditional X.509.
What I'm questioning is your assertion that collision attacks are the only class of attacks that the randomness is protecting against.
My intuition is telling me that there are some Merle tree specific attacks that the randomness is also helping with (ie randomness helps with attacks that don't have regular-X.509 analogs).

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: Oh I see. Not sure I'm following your attack, but maybe I'm not reading it right. You could certainly compute what the inclusion proof would have been if the next entry is xyz. But that inclusion proof will lead to a root hash that presumably wasn't signed by the CA.

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: what's the other attack, Mike?

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: (Or a subtree. But one way or another, we only accept the tree hashes that are somehow authenticated by the CA.)

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: @Mike Ounsworth To be very clear, the randomizer is not necessary for MTC as-is at 128-bit security level, because SHA2 is a good hash.

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
Daniel Gillmor said:

what's the other attack, Mike?

I don't know. Maybe (hopefully) there isn't one.
My ENTIRE point here is that since the details of the randomness scheme (including the security model of the attacks it's intending to protect against) doesn't seem to have been written down yet, it's a bit difficult to construct an attack against it, or state that there isn't one. :yum:

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: The randomizer lives in a weird place. The kinds of people that have weird excessive requirements are going to want a different hash anyway (IMO)

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: By the way, Andy Hülsing who replied in the thread, is the world expert on this kind of stuff.
